{
    refine' fun i =>
      calc
        ⋂ (j : ι) (_ : j ≥ i), (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ ↦ M_ i) j
          = ⋂ (j : ι) (_ : j ≥ i), Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ ↦ M_ j := by rfl
        _ ≤ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun a ↦ ⋂ (j : ι) (_ : j ≥ i), M_ j := by
          -- This is true because intersection commutes with product
          apply Submodule.pi_mono
          intro a _
          simp [iInf_iInf]
        _ ≤ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun a ↦ M_ i := by
          -- Because for each a, ⋂_{j ≥ i} M_j ≤ M_i since M_ is IsFilterComplete
          apply Submodule.pi_mono
          intro a _
          have h : ⋂ (j : ι) (_ : j ≥ i), M_ j ≤ M_ i := by
            -- Use the IsFilterComplete property of M_
            have h' : ∀ (j : ι), j ≥ i → ⋂ (j : ι) (_ : j ≥ i), M_ j ≤ M_ j := by
              intro j hj
              apply iInf_le
              <;> aesop
            -- Now get ⋂_{j ≥ i} M_j ≤ M_i
            have h'' : IsFilterComplete M_ := by infer_instance
            exact h''.le_iInf i
          exact h
        _ = (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ ↦ M_ i) i := by rfl
  }

have h_main : IsFilterComplete (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) := by
    -- We need to show that the function (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) is filter complete.
    -- This means that for any filter 𝓝, there exists an index j such that for all i ≥ j, the image of i under the function is in 𝓝.
    -- However, in this case, we can directly use the fact that the family M_ is filter complete and the structure of Submodule.pi.
    -- The key observation is that Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i is the product of M_ i over all elements of α.
    -- Since the product of a filter complete family is filter complete, the result follows.
    exact inferInstance
  exact h_main

have h_main : ∀ (s : Set ι), ∃ (t : Finset ι), (⋂ i ∈ s, Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) = (⋂ i ∈ (t : Set ι), Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) := by
    intro s
    -- Use the filter completeness of M_ to get a finite set t such that ⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i = ⋂ i ∈ t, M_ i
    have h₁ : ∃ (t : Finset ι), (⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i) = ⋂ i ∈ (t : Set ι), M_ i := by
      -- Use the filter completeness property of M_
      have h₂ : ∃ (t : Finset ι), (⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i) = ⋂ i ∈ (t : Set ι), M_ i := by
        -- Use the fact that M_ is filter complete to get the required finite set
        have h₃ : ∃ (t : Finset ι), (⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i) = ⋂ i ∈ (t : Set ι), M_ i := by
          -- This is directly from the definition of IsFilterComplete
          exact IsFilterComplete.exists_finset_eq (M_ := M_) s
        -- The result follows from the previous step
        exact h₃
      -- The result follows from the previous step
      exact h₂
    -- Obtain the finite set t
    obtain ⟨t, ht⟩ := h₁
    -- Show that ⋂ i ∈ s, Submodule.pi ... = ⋂ i ∈ t, Submodule.pi ...
    refine' ⟨t, _⟩
    -- Use the distributivity of intersection over product to simplify the expression
    calc
      (⋂ i ∈ s, Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) = Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ ⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i := by
        -- Apply the lemma to distribute the intersection over the product
        rw [Submodule.pi_inf_distrib]
        <;> simp [Set.mem_univ]
        <;> aesop
      _ = Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ ⋂ i ∈ (t : Set ι), M_ i := by
        -- Substitute the equality ⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i = ⋂ i ∈ t, M_ i
        congr 1
        ext x
        rw [ht]
      _ = (⋂ i ∈ (t : Set ι), Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) := by
        -- Apply the lemma to distribute the intersection over the product in the reverse direction
        rw [Submodule.pi_inf_distrib]
        <;> simp [Set.mem_univ]
        <;> aesop
        <;>
        (try simp_all)
        <;>
        (try aesop)
  -- Use the main lemma to conclude the proof
  exact inferInstance

refine' ⟨fun ⟨r, hr⟩ => ⟨r, _⟩⟩
  -- We need to show that for any subset `r` of `ι`, there exists a directed set `r'` such that `r ≤ r'`.
  -- In this case, we can take `r' = r` because the family `M_` is already a filter.
  -- The `pi` operation preserves the filter property.
  refine' by
    dsimp only at hr ⊢
    -- Simplify the goal to show that the `pi` of the submodules is contained in the `pi` of the filter.
    -- This follows directly from the fact that the filter is preserved under the `pi` operation.
    exact by
      simpa [Submodule.pi_le] using hr

    exact ⟨h₁, h₂⟩
⟩

formalize this in Lean, we can use that the intersection of submodules is the product of the intersections. 

Thus, the intersection of (pi_{x in α} M_i) over i is equal to pi_{x in α} (intersection_i M_i). 

But since (M_i) is filter complete, the intersection of M_i is zero. 

Hence, the product of the intersections is pi_{x in α} 0 = 0. 

Therefore, the intersection of (pi_{x in α} M_i) is zero, and we are done.

But we need to formalize this reasoning in Lean. 

Actually, perhaps we can find an existing lemma in Mathlib that would help, but looking at the available imports, it might not be there. 

Alternatively, we can directly use the fact that the intersection of products is the product of intersections, as follows:

{ have h : ∀ i j : ι, i ≤ j → (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) i ≤ (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i) j := by
      intro i j hij
      have : ∀ x : α, M_ i ≤ M_ j := by
        intro x
        exact M_.mono hij
      exact Submodule.pi_mono fun x => this x
    refine' ⟨h, _⟩
    let s : ι → Submodule k (M ^ α) := fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun _ : α ↦ M_ i
    -- Use the fact that the product of the filtered modules is filtered
    have h₂ : ∀ (F : Filter ι), ∀ (i : ι), F ≤ nhds i → (∀ j ∈ F, s j ≤ s i) → (∀ (j : ι), s j ≤ s i) := by
      intro F i hF h_le
      have h₃ : ∀ (j : ι), s j ≤ s i := by
        intro j
        have h₄ : j ≤ i ∨ i ≤ j := by
          cases' le_total j i with h h
          · exact Or.inl h
          · exact Or.inr h
        cases' h₄ with h₄ h₄
        · have h₅ : s j ≤ s i := by
            have : j ≤ i := h₄
            exact h_le i (Filter.mem_nhds_of_le hF (Filter.le_refl i)) this
          exact h₅
        · have h₅ : s j ≤ s i := by
            have : i ≤ j := h₄
            have h₆ : s i ≤ s j := by
              have : ∀ x : α, M_ i ≤ M_ j := by
                intro x
                exact M_.mono this
              exact Submodule.pi_mono fun x => this x
            have h₇ : s j ≤ s i := by
              have h₈ : j ∈ F := by
                have h₉ : i ∈ F := Filter.mem_of_superset (Filter.mem_nhds_of_le hF (Filter.le_refl i)) (by intro x hx; simp_all [le_of_eq])
                have h₁₀ : i ≤ j := this
                exact Filter.mem_of_superset h₉ (Filter.le_of_subset (fun x hx => by simp_all [le_of_eq]))
              exact h_le j h₈
            exact le_antisymm h₆ h₇
          exact h₅
      exact h₃
    -- Use the fact that the product of the filtered modules is filtered
    have h₃ : ∀ (F : Filter ι), ∀ (i : ι), F ≤ nhds i → (∀ j ∈ F, s j ≤ s i) := by
      intro F i hF
      have h₄ : ∀ j ∈ F, s j ≤ s i := by
        intro j hj
        have : j ≤ i := by
          have : j ∈ F := hj
          have : F ≤ nhds i := hF
          exact Filter.mem_nhds.mp (hF hj)
        have : s j ≤ s i := by
          have : ∀ x : α, M_ j ≤ M_ i := by
            intro x
            exact M_.mono this
          exact Submodule.pi_mono fun x => this x
        exact this
      exact h₄
    -- Use the fact that the product of the filtered modules is filtered
    refine' ⟨fun F i hF => ⟨_, _⟩⟩ <;> simp_all [h₂, h₃]
    <;> aesop }

by
  unfold IsFilterComplete
  -- We need to show that the intersection of the submodules over all i is zero.
  -- This follows from the fact that each M_i is a submodule and the filter completeness of M_.
  have h₁ : Filter.Comap (fun i : ι => (fun x => M_ i) x) (⊥ : Filter (M →₀ k)) = ⊥ := by
    -- The filter completeness of M_ implies that the intersection of M_i is zero.
    -- Therefore, the intersection of the product submodules is also zero.
    have h₂ : ∀ x : α, Filter.Comap (fun i : ι => (fun x => M_ i) x) (⊥ : Filter (M →₀ k)) = ⊥ := by
      intro x
      simp [Filter.comap_bot]
    -- Since the filter completeness holds for each x, it holds for the product.
    have h₃ : Filter.Comap (fun i : ι => (fun x => M_ i) x) (⊥ : Filter (M →₀ k)) = ⊥ := by
      -- Use the fact that the filter completeness holds for each x to deduce it for the product.
      have h₄ : Filter.Comap (fun i : ι => (fun x => M_ i) x) (⊥ : Filter (M →₀ k)) = ⊥ := by
        simp [Filter.comap_bot]
      exact h₄
    exact h₃
  -- Use the fact that the intersection of the product submodules is zero to deduce the filter completeness.
  simpa [Filter.comap_bot] using h₁

        intro x _
        trivial
      -- Use the fact that each M_ is IsFilterComplete to show that each component is 0
      have h₃ : ∀ (a : α), (∀ (i : ι), i ∈ s → x a ∈ (M_ i : Set M)) := by
        intro a
        intro i hi
        have h₄ : x ∈ Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α ↦ ⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i) := hx
        have h₅ : x a ∈ (⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i : Submodule k M) := by
          have h₆ : x ∈ Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α ↦ ⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i) := hx
          have h₇ : x a ∈ (⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i : Submodule k M) := by
            have h₈ : x ∈ Submodule.pi Set.univ (fun _ : α ↦ ⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i) := hx
            have h₉ : (x : α → M) a ∈ (fun _ : α ↦ ⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i) a := by
              apply Submodule.mem_pi.mp h₈
              <;> aesop
            simpa using h₉
          exact h₇
        have h₆ : x a ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
          have h₇ : x a ∈ (⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i : Submodule k M) := h₅
          have h₈ : x a ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
            have h₉ : i ∈ s := hi
            have h₁₀ : x a ∈ (⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i : Submodule k M) := h₅
            have h₁₁ : x a ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
              have h₁₂ : (⋂ i ∈ s, M_ i : Submodule k M) ≤ M_ i := by
                apply Submodule.iInf_le
                <;> aesop
              exact h₁₂ h₁₀
            exact h₁₁
          exact h₈
        exact h₆
      -- Use the IsFilterComplete property to show that each component is 0
      have h₄ : ∀ (a : α), x a = 0 := by
        intro a
        have h₅ : ∀ (i : ι), i ∈ s → x a ∈ (M_ i : Set M) := by
          intro i hi
          exact h₃ a i hi
        have h₆ : x a ∈ ⋂ i ∈ s, (M_ i : Set M) := by
          intro i hi
          exact h₅ i hi
        have h₇ : ⋂ i ∈ s, (M_ i : Set M) = {0} := by
          have h₈ : IsFilterComplete M_ := inferInstance
          have h₉ : ∀ (i : ι), i ∈ s → (M_ i : Set M) ∈ Set.univ := by
            intro i hi
            trivial
          have h₁₀ : ⋂ i ∈ s, (M_ i : Set M) = {0} := by
            apply h₈
            <;> aesop
          exact h₁₀
        rw [h₇] at h₆
        simpa using h₆
      -- Since each component is 0, x = 0
      have h₅ : x = 0 := by
        ext a
        have h₆ : x a = 0 := h₄ a
        simpa using h₆
      simpa using h₅
    · -- Prove the reverse direction: if x = 0, then x is in the product of intersections
      intro hx
      have h₁ : x = 0 := hx
      rw [h₁]
      intro a ha
      simp_all [Submodule.mem_bot]
      <;>
      aesop
  }

have h₁ : ∀ i, (fun i ↦ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun x : α ↦ M_ i) i = Submodule.pi Set.univ fun x : α ↦ M_ i := by simp
  have h₂ : ∀ i j, i ≤ j → Submodule.pi Set.univ fun x : α ↦ M_ i ≤ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun x : α ↦ M_ j := by
    intro i j hij
    have h₃ : ∀ x : α, M_ i ≤ M_ j := by
      intro x
      exact M_.mono hij
    exact Submodule.pi_mono Set.univ fun x : α => h₃ x
  have h₃ : ∀ (s : Set ι), s.Nonempty → BddAbove s → ∃ i ∈ s, ∀ j ∈ s, i ≤ j → Submodule.pi Set.univ fun x : α ↦ M_ i ≤ Submodule.pi Set.univ fun x : α ↦ M_ j := by
    intro s hs hbs
    have h₄ : ∃ i ∈ s, ∀ j ∈ s, i ≤ j := by
      classical
      -- Use the classical approach to find a maximal element in the set s
      have h₅ : ∃ (i : ι), i ∈ s ∧ ∀ (j : ι), j ∈ s → i ≤ j := by
        -- Use the fact that the set is bounded above and nonempty to find a maximal element
        classical
        -- Use the fact that the set is bounded above and nonempty to find a maximal element
        have h₆ : ∃ i ∈ s, ∀ j ∈ s, i ≤ j := by
          -- Use the classical choice principle to find a maximal element
          classical
          -- Use the classical choice principle to find a maximal element
          exact ⟨s.max' (by simpa using hs), by
            exact Set.max'_mem s _
            , fun j hj => by
              exact Set.le_max' s j hj
              ⟩
        -- Use the classical choice principle to find a maximal element
        exact h₆
      -- Use the classical approach to find a maximal element in the set s
      obtain ⟨i, hi, hij⟩ := h₅
      refine' ⟨i, hi, fun j hj => _⟩
      -- Use the fact that i is a maximal element to conclude that i ≤ j for all j in s
      exact hij j hj
    -- Use the maximal element to show that the set of submodules is bounded above
    obtain ⟨i, hi, hi'⟩ := h₄
    refine' ⟨i, hi, fun j hj hij => _⟩
    -- Use the monotonicity of the submodules to show that the set of submodules is bounded above
    exact h₂ i j hij
  -- Use the fact that the set of submodules is bounded above to conclude that the set of submodules is bounded above
  infer_instance

by
  have h : IsFilterComplete (fun i : ι => Submodule.pi (Set.univ : Set α) (fun x : α => M_ i)) := by
    apply inferInstance
  exact h

⟨fun b ↦ ⟨Classical.choose b, by
    have h : ∀ (i : ι), (Classical.choose b (i : ι)).1 ∈ M_ i :=
      by
        intro i
        exact (Classical.choose_spec b (i : ι)).1
    have h₁ : ∀ (i : ι), (Classical.choose b (i : ι)).1 ∈ M_ i := h
    have h₂ : ∀ (a : α), (∑ i : ι, (Classical.choose b (i : ι))) a ∈ ⨅ i : ι, M_ i :=
      by
        intro a
        apply iInf_mem
        <;> simp_all [Submodule.coe_sum]
        <;> exact λ i => by
          have h₃ : (Classical.choose b i).1 ∈ M_ i := h₁ i
          simpa using h₃
      <;> simp_all [Submodule.coe_sum]
      <;> exact λ i => by
        have h₃ : (Classical.choose b i).1 ∈ M_ i := h₁ i
        simpa using h₃
    simp_all [Submodule.mem_pi]
    <;> aesop
  ⟩, fun b ↦ by
    have h : ∀ (i : ι), (Classical.choose b (i : ι)).1 ∈ M_ i :=
      by
        intro i
        exact (Classical.choose_spec b (i : ι)).1
    have h₁ : ∀ (i : ι), (Classical.choose b (i : ι)).1 ∈ M_ i := h
    have h₂ : ∀ (a : α), (∑ i : ι, (Classical.choose b (i : ι))) a ∈ ⨅ i : ι, M_ i :=
      by
        intro a
        apply iInf_mem
        <;> simp_all [Submodule.coe_sum]
        <;> exact λ i => by
          have h₃ : (Classical.choose b i).1 ∈ M_ i := h₁ i
          simpa using h₃
      <;> simp_all [Submodule.coe_sum]
      <;> exact λ i => by
        have h₃ : (Classical.choose b i).1 ∈ M_ i := h₁ i
        simpa using h₃
    simp_all [Submodule.mem_pi]
    <;> aesop
  ⟩
